intended_role: Cross-functional project manager for implementation programs

organization.role_title: Cross-functional project manager for implementation programs

contextual_signals.best_role_ever: Matrix environment where project authority is respected and decisions have owners.
contextual_signals.worst_role_ever: Ambiguous organization that expects coordination without authority.
contextual_signals.current_energy_drain: Stakeholders who agree in meetings and miss commitments afterward.
contextual_signals.recurring_org_frustration: Can get trapped carrying coordination debt instead of forcing ownership decisions.
contextual_signals.relied_on_for: Keeps cross-functional work moving by clarifying ownership and surfacing slippage early.
contextual_signals.misunderstood_for: Can get trapped carrying coordination debt instead of forcing ownership decisions.
contextual_signals.unrealized_capacity: Strong project manager fit; needs escalation rights to avoid becoming the buffer.

q2.text: I want stability, progress, and a career where people trust me to make complicated work feel manageable. I care about relationships but also about commitments meaning something. Long term I want to lead bigger programs without becoming the person who absorbs every miss.

q14.text: A launch milestone slipped because two teams assumed the other owned a dependency. I rebuilt the timeline, named the owner, and added a dependency review to the weekly meeting. The miss showed that agreement without ownership is not enough.

q17.text: People would say I become organized and persistent under pressure. I follow up, clarify, and make the next step visible. The weakness is that I may carry too much coordination myself before escalating.

q20.text: I had to make a schedule call before engineering had final estimates. I created a range, identified the risk, and communicated the decision to stakeholders. Afterward I was glad the uncertainty was explicit instead of hidden.

q22.text: I would rate myself a 7. I create clarity and keep people aligned, but I need to be firmer sooner when someone misses an owner commitment.

q24.text: When momentum stalls I find the stuck dependency and ask who owns the next move. I get frustrated by vague agreement and quiet misses. I avoid escalating too early because I want teams to solve things collaboratively.

q25.text: I restate the goal and the agreed next step. I try to clarify without making the person feel blamed.

q26.text: I naturally become the coordinator, translator, and progress keeper. Tension appears when I am accountable for delivery but do not control the functional resources.

q27.text: I am building a career around trusted execution and useful leadership. I value clarity, reliability, and respectful accountability.

q28.text: Project plans, RAID logs, meeting notes, and dependency trackers keep work organized. Future strain appears if program complexity grows without stronger decision rights.
